notes: Entity confirmed. birthYear 1930 / deathYear 2017 match (Sept 19, 1930 - Oct 29, 2017). 'Founding president of the AACM in 1965' matches evidence ('founding member and the first president', elected president at the second meeting in 1965). Experimental Band leadership confirmed, and Roscoe Mitchell's involvement is supported by his quote about Abrams. Not checkable from the evidence: that the Experimental Band was the training ground for Anthony Braxton (evidence ties Braxton to Abrams only via his recording debut on Abrams' 1967 album Levels and Degrees of Light) and for Henry Threadgill (not mentioned anywhere in the extract). No contradictions found.

Bio is a verbatim copy of the Wikipedia lead paragraph; every claim (church playing, Ramsey Lewis then Oscar Peterson influence, McCoy Tyner harmonic freedom, hard bop, influence from the 1980s on) appears word-for-word in the evidence. birthYear 1955 / deathYear 2013 match (Aug 13, 1955 - May 29, 2013). Piano confirmed.

Bio is a verbatim copy of the Wikipedia lead. birthYear 1931 / deathYear 2000 match (Nov 25, 1931 - Jan 2, 2000). Trumpet and composer confirmed ('American jazz trumpeter and composer'). Minor context, not an error: evidence says he switched to cornet in 1950 and preferred it; our bio makes no contrary claim.

Bio is a verbatim copy of the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified, birthYear 1919 / deathYear 1965 match (Mar 17, 1919 - Feb 15, 1965). Data-quality note (not an error): instruments field is just ['musician'], which is vague; evidence supports 'singer' and 'jazz pianist' as concrete values.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1967 matches (Feb 17, 1967), living, flute/composer confirmed. Black Earth Ensemble and 'Mandorla Awakening II' (FPE, 2016) confirmed. Two claims not checkable: (1) ours says 'former president of the AACM' while the evidence says 'former chairwoman of the AACM' -- a different title for what is plausibly the same office; not a proven contradiction, but a repair-writer should align our wording with the source ('chairwoman'); (2) the 'Ice Crystal' project is not mentioned anywhere in the evidence. Afrofuturist/sci-fi framing is editorial.

Bio is a verbatim copy of the Wikipedia lead (NPM nickname, Norwegian trumpeter/composer/producer, future jazz pioneer, Khmer as most commercially successful album). birthYear 1960 matches (Sept 18, 1960), living. Trumpet confirmed.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1933 / deathYear 2003 match (Feb 21, 1933 - Apr 21, 2003); piano and vocals confirmed. 'High Priestess of Soul' verbatim in evidence; classical training (Juilliard, Curtis application) and genre breadth confirmed; civil rights activism confirmed. The one substantive claim not checkable: 'Mississippi Goddam' does not appear in the truncated extract, so the specific song-title claim is unverified (consistent with the civil-rights framing, no contradiction).

wrongClaims.ourClaim: His 1959 debut 'The Shape of Jazz to Come'
wrongClaims.problem: The Shape of Jazz to Come (1959, Atlantic) was his third studio album, not his debut; his debut was Something Else!!!! (1958, Contemporary)
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: In 1959, Atlantic Records released Coleman's third studio album, The Shape of Jazz to Come.

notes: Right entity throughout; birthYear 1930 / deathYear 2015 match (Mar 9, 1930 - Jun 11, 2015); alto sax/violin/trumpet all confirmed. Free Jazz (1960) giving the genre its name, plastic (Grafton) alto, self-taught, Prime Time electric band, and harmolodics all confirmed. The single error is calling The Shape of Jazz to Come his 'debut' -- evidence also states 'his debut album, Something Else!!!! (1958)'. A repair could fix it by saying '1959 Atlantic breakthrough' or similar. 'Influenced everyone from Pat Metheny to punk rockers' is not checkable from this evidence.

Bio is a verbatim copy of the Wikipedia lead (200+ recordings, eight Grammys, lifetime achievement award, 60+ year career, 'Maharaja of the keyboard', 'O.P.', 'King of inside swing'); all confirmed. birthYear 1925 / deathYear 2007 match. Data-quality flag (omission, not a contradiction): instruments field lists only 'composer' and omits piano -- his defining instrument per evidence ('was a Canadian jazz pianist and composer'). Repair-writer should add piano.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: brain surgery for a near-fatal aneurysm in 1980
wrongClaims.problem: Evidence names the 1980 condition as a hemorrhaged arteriovenous malformation (AVM), a different condition from an aneurysm
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: In 1980, Martino suffered a hemorrhaged arteriovenous malformation that caused a "near-fatal seizure".

notes: Right entity; birthYear 1944 / deathYear 2021 match (Aug 25, 1944 - Nov 1, 2021); guitar confirmed. The 1980 surgery, total amnesia, and complete relearning of the guitar are all confirmed -- only the diagnosis differs ('aneurysm' vs the evidence's 'arteriovenous malformation'). Many popular accounts do say aneurysm, so this may be a common simplification rather than a scrape corruption; flagging at moderate confidence so a human can decide whether to reword to match the source. 'Played at the highest level for decades after recovery' is consistent (recordings through 2017; stopped performing 2018).

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1954 matches (Aug 12, 1954), living, guitar confirmed. 'Bright Size Life' as debut at 21 checks out (debut album, recorded Dec 1975 / released 1976; he was 21). Pat Metheny Group with key collaborator Lyle Mays confirmed; multiple Grammys confirmed (20). Not checkable from the truncated extract: the Orchestrion robot-orchestra project and the claim that he explored Ornette Coleman's harmolodics (neither appears in the evidence text). No contradictions.
Bio is a verbatim copy of the Wikipedia lead (hard bop rhythm-section fixture, known for bowed solos). birthYear 1935 / deathYear 1969 match (Apr 22, 1935 - Jan 4, 1969). Double bass confirmed.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1941 / deathYear 2023 match (Mar 6, 1941 - Jun 22, 2023). Saxophone and clarinet confirmed ('German jazz saxophonist and clarinetist'; tenor/alto split in our field is finer-grained than the evidence but within the confirmed instrument family). Machine Gun (1968) confirmed; 'most intense free jazz recording ever made' is editorial superlative -- evidence's 'one of the landmark albums of 20th-century free jazz' is compatible, not contradicting. 50+ year career consistent (1967 first recording to 2023 death). 'Godfather of European free improvisation' is editorial, supported by 'central and pioneering figure in European free jazz'.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1940 / deathYear 2022 match (Oct 13, 1940 - Sept 24, 2022); tenor saxophone confirmed (soprano not named but generic 'saxophonist' covers it). 'The Creator Has a Master Plan' confirmed verbatim. Not checkable: the 'late-career renaissance with Floating Points' -- the extract truncates in the 2000s and never mentions Floating Points (the 2021 Promises collaboration). No contradictions.

Bio is a verbatim copy of the Wikipedia lead and early-biography sentences (Springfield MA, saxophone at 12, Harvey LaRose, Benny Carter and Johnny Hodges as heroes); all confirmed. birthYear 1931 / deathYear 2015 match. Data-quality flag (omission, not a contradiction): instruments field is ['clarinet','composer','bandleader'] and omits alto saxophone -- his primary instrument per the evidence AND per our own bio text ('American jazz alto saxophonist, clarinetist, bandleader, and composer'). Repair-writer should add alto saxophone.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1935 matches (Apr 20, 1935), living per evidence, piano confirmed. fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: The group was active from 1970-1977, and reunited briefly in 2004.

notes: Right entity. Personnel fully confirmed verbatim (violinist Leroy Jenkins, bassist Sirone, drummer/percussionist Jerome Cooper); 'active primarily in the 1970s' and the democratic/leaderless cooperative ethos are confirmed ('it was a cooperative group'; 'We're not talking about no leaders here'). The error: our birthYear (formation year) is 1971, but evidence twice states formation/activity from 1970 ('Prior to the formation of the Revolutionary Ensemble in 1970'). Also for the repair-writer: deathYear null reads as 'still active', but the evidence says the group disbanded in 1977, reunited 2004-2005, and Jenkins's 2007 death 'precluded any further reunions'. 'Loft jazz movement' label is not in the evidence (though Artists House loft rehearsals are); 'without a chordal instrument' matches the primary violin/bass/drums instrumentation, though Cooper doubled on piano.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1978 matches (Apr 5, 1978), living, piano/keyboards confirmed. Church upbringing confirmed (mother music director at East Wind Baptist Church; 'first developed his sound in church'); Black Radio with Erykah Badu confirmed; The Experiment ensemble confirmed; jazz/hip-hop/R&B bridge confirmed (Best R&B Album Grammy, To Pimp a Butterfly keyboards). Not checkable: (1) 'Growing up in Houston' -- the evidence places his schooling in Missouri City, Texas and HSPVA but never names Houston; (2) the phrasing implies Kendrick Lamar appears on a Black Radio album -- the evidence's guest lists for Black Radio and Black Radio 2 do not include Kendrick (his Kendrick connection in evidence is playing on To Pimp a Butterfly). Neither is contradicted outright; suggest rewording the Kendrick clause.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1950 matches (May 12, 1950), living, organ confirmed ('American funk and soul jazz organist'); Buffalo NY birthplace confirmed verbatim; the A Tribe Called Quest 'Electric Relaxation' sample of 'Mystic Brew' confirmed verbatim. Not checkable: that his Blue Note debut was in 1972 (evidence only says 'albums recorded for Blue Note Records in the 1970s' -- the extract's discography section is empty) and that 'Mystic Brew' is from that debut album. 'One of the most sampled songs in hip-hop history' is editorial hyperbole; evidence documents ATCQ and J. Cole samples. No contradictions.

Entity confirmed; birthYear 1940 matches (Aug 3, 1940), living. Co-founder of both AACM and Art Ensemble of Chicago confirmed verbatim; 'Sound' as 1966 first studio album confirmed ('a departure from the more extroverted work of the New York-based free jazz players', recorded with 'unorthodox devices'), consistent with our texture/extended-techniques framing; 5+ decade career consistent. The only unchecked claim is the 'flute' entry in instruments -- the evidence confirms saxophone, clarinet, and (via the Art Ensemble) percussion, but never mentions flute. No contradictions.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Roy Edward Ayers Jr. (September 10, 1940 - March 4, 2025) was an American vibraphonist, record producer, and composer.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Now in his 80s, he continues performing
wrongClaims.problem: Ayers is dead (March 4, 2025) and per the evidence had already stopped performing live in 2023; the present-tense claim is no longer true
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Ayers died at a hospital in Manhattan, New York, on March 4, 2025, at the age of 84, after suffering from a long illness.

notes: Right entity; birthYear 1940 and vibraphone confirmed. Ubiquity jazz-funk, 'Everybody Loves the Sunshine', 'Running Away', and heavy hip-hop sampling all confirmed ('listed among the performers whose music was most often sampled by rappers'). The errors are staleness, not wrong-entity corruption: deathYear should be 2025 and the 'continues performing' sentence needs rewriting. Also note: evidence says he 'continued to perform live until 2023'. Unverified minor details: Milt Jackson influence (evidence credits Lionel Hampton with his first mallets) and ATCQ/Mary J. Blige as specific samplers (evidence names Jill Scott, Guru, J. Cole contexts).
